title: Rumohra hasseltii
intro: Rumohra hasseltii, also known as the maidenhair fern, is a species of flowering plant in the family Blechnaceae, native to tropical Asia. The plant is valued for its potential health benefits, including anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties, which may help to alleviate symptoms of arthritis and protect against oxidative stress. Bioactive constituents of Rumohra hasseltii include flavonoids, phenolic acids, and saponins, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. In traditional herbal medicine, the plant is often used to make teas, infusions, and tinctures, which are used to treat a range of health conditions, including fever, cough, and respiratory issues.
taxonomy: Rumohra hasseltii is classified within the family Dryopteridaceae, commonly known as the wood fern or male fern family. The genus Rumohra belongs to the division Pteridophyta, which comprises ferns. This classification reflects the plant's characteristics and its evolutionary relationships to other ferns. The species Rumohra hasseltii is native to tropical Asia and has been naturalized in other parts of the world. As a member of the Dryopteridaceae family, Rumohra hasseltii exhibits a unique combination of morphological and anatomical features.
uses: Rumohra hasseltii is traditionally used in Southeast Asian medicine to treat various health issues. Its rhizomes are believed to possess anti-inflammatory and antiseptic properties. In some cultures, it is used to treat fever, rheumatism, and gastrointestinal problems. Additionally, the plant's leaves are sometimes used to treat respiratory issues such as bronchitis and asthma. Further research is needed to confirm the effectiveness of Rumohra hasseltii for medicinal purposes.

side_effects: Rumohra hasseltii, also known as the fern, is generally considered non-toxic and safe for use in gardens and indoor spaces. While there are no reported severe side effects, it's essential to handle the plant with care and avoid accidental ingestion.
